Building something real.
I am currently developing Appointix, an AI-powered appointment engine for life insurance advisers.
This is a live software product moving toward launch.
There is one problem I keep seeing in the industry.
Advisers are strong at closing.
But most do not have a consistent pipeline of qualified appointments.
The result:
• unpredictable income
• too much time spent chasing leads
• low-quality conversations
• pressure to constantly restart the pipeline
So I focused on fixing that.
Appointix applies AI directly inside the workflow to:
• initiate and manage prospect outreach
• qualify conversations before they reach the adviser
• book confirmed appointments into the calendar
• maintain engagement to improve attendance
The objective is simple.
Create a steady flow of real, qualified conversations without manual chasing.
This is not about adding AI for the sake of it.
It is about using AI to execute a structured process that produces measurable outcomes.
Current progress:
• end-to-end workflow designed and running
• AI-driven engagement system active
• multi-step contact approach across messaging and voice
• operator control panel in development
• preparing for pilot with live users
Appointix will be available shortly for advisers who want a more consistent and scalable way to generate appointments.

I am also open to:
• freelance opportunities
• contract roles
• product and AI-focused projects
If you are working on similar challenges, hiring, or want early access, feel free to reach out.

Workday Isn't Working

Workday is facing a class action lawsuit alleging that its AI hiring tool discriminated against possibly millions of applicants. The case targets Workday directly, not just the employers using the platform.

This is a human-in-the-loop failure at scale.
HR professionals had observable data. When your applicant pool skews consistently toward candidates in their 20s and 30s, that is a signal. Someone saw it. The right governance framework would have required someone to act on it.

It is also a procurement failure. Organizations did not ask Workday the right questions before deploying the tool. How does the agent make decisions? How is each version validated before rollout? What bias testing was performed and by whom? Those are governance questions, and a mature governance framework forces those conversations before a contract is signed, not after a lawsuit is filed.

What makes this case significant beyond Workday is the scale. When a single platform manages hiring for thousands of organizations, one governance failure does not stay contained. It replicates.

If your organization uses a third-party AI tool to screen, rank, or filter candidates, the governance responsibility does not transfer to the vendor. It stays with you.

What procurement questions is your organization asking before signing an AI vendor contract?

Remote jobs are not gone. You are just searching the wrong words.

I use Perplexity to find global remote jobs that normal filters miss.

A lot of companies do not say:
“work from anywhere”

They use other words like:
• contract
• freelance
• global team
• work across time zones
• anywhere
• outside the US
• remote in selected countries

That small change matters.

Here is the simple workflow:

1/ Search for hiring signs
In Perplexity, prompt:

“Find companies hiring for [your role] that may accept people from many countries. Look for words like contract, freelance, global team, work across time zones, anywhere, or outside the US. Skip jobs that clearly need US-only hiring. Show me the company, job link, country limits, job type, and why it may fit.”

Now you are not only searching “remote.”
You are searching how companies really write it.

2/ Check the company pages
Then ask:

“Check company career pages, founder posts, and hiring pages for this role. Find companies that look open to global remote workers, even if the job title does not say work from anywhere.”

This helps you find jobs filters miss.

3/ Save the good ones
Put the results into Notion or Google Sheets.

Track:
• company
• role
• link
• country limit
• time zone need
• job type
• notes

4/ Match your CV to that kind of job
Use ChatGPT or Claude.
Ask it to rewrite your best CV points for:
• working alone
• clear writing
• fast work
• working with people in different time zones
• real results

The best global remote jobs are often not under the “remote” filter.
They are hidden behind simple words.

AI helps you find them faster.

Is your AI Agent an 'Employee' or a 'computer system'?

The emergency talks this morning between the FCA, Bank of England, and the NCSC over 'Script Fatigue' and software risks are a stark reminder of the digital frontier we’re crossing. For me, the real risk isn't just the use of agents or the code—it’s the Accountability Gap.

​I was struck by a theme at a recent Deloitte HIA event: the idea that we should be treating AI Agents more like employees than computer systems. ​In a traditional control cycle, roles and responsibilities are the bedrock of assurance. Yet, as we deploy autonomous agents to monitor, test, and even execute controls, we are seeing a dangerous decoupling. The person who 'owns' the control cycle often doesn't 'own' the AI Agent operating it. If the AI develops 'Script Fatigue'—missing glitches because its autonomous testing has become too predictable—who is truly responsible?

​Under Provision 29, the Board’s declaration of effectiveness must be underpinned by a clear line of sight. If we treat AI Agents as 'black box' IT systems, we lose the 'Operational Grip' that the regulator is looking for.

​My key questions are:
​1) Does every AI Agent in your control environment have a named business owner (not just an IT lead)?
​2) Are we auditing the 'intent' and 'judgment' of our AI Agents with the same rigor we apply to our human teams?
​3) If an AI Agent fails to flag a material breach today, is it immediately clear whose desk that failure lands on?
4) If your AI Agent made a materially wrong decision tomorrow, would your current R&R framework know who to hold accountable?

​Governance is about managing systems and, now, the Digital Workforce as well. Are we hiring AI 'employees' without a contract of accountability?

Illinois just passed a law requiring employers to disclose when AI is used in hiring.

It's structured backwards. Twice.

Here's the first inversion.
Human hiring decisions are demonstrably biased. Resume name studies. Interview rating variance. Callback rates by zip code. Decades of replicated research.

AI hiring tools have bias problems too, usually inherited from the same humans who built them.

But here's the difference: AI is auditable. You can test it. Measure it. Find the bias and, in principle, fix it.

A 45-minute interview is not auditable. It leaves no log. No data. No way to isolate why one candidate was rated higher than another.

We are adding transparency requirements to the system we can inspect, while leaving the system we can't inspect completely unregulated.

That's the first inversion.

Here's the second one.
The law puts the compliance burden on the employer.
But the employer didn't build the AI. The vendor did.

The employer doesn't know how the model scores candidates. The vendor does.

The employer can't explain why one profile ranked above another. The vendor might not be able to either.

But the law says: employer, disclose. Not: vendor, explain.

That's holding the party with the least visibility legally accountable for a system they didn't build and can't fully inspect.

These two inversions point at the same gap. We're regulating the outputs of a system without requiring anyone to explain how it works. And we're assigning accountability to the party furthest from the answer.

There's one way this law produces real change despite both flaws.
If employers become legally liable for AI they don't understand, they will start demanding that vendors explain it. Contracts will change. Procurement questions will change. Vendors who can't answer will lose deals to vendors who can.

That's an indirect mechanism. It's slow. But it might be the only lever that actually moves anything.

The question isn't whether AI should be disclosed in hiring.

It's whether disclosure, without explainability, without vendor accountability, protects anyone at all.

Or whether it just produces a new kind of checkbox that everyone clicks through.

Who do you think should be legally accountable when an AI hiring tool gets it wrong. The employer who bought it, or the vendor who built it?

Finance Controller is the most brutal job in finance. Quiet. Unfashionable. Often invisible. When things go right, no one notices. When something breaks, everyone asks.

I was speaking with a CFO recently about what he looks for when hiring a Finance Controller. And his answer had nothing to do with AI. Or automation.
It just came down to three things.

3. Unglamorous fundamentals

Everyone wants the modern finance leader. AI. Automation. Dashboards. Strategic insights. But none of that matters if the foundation is weak.

Controllers are the people who make sure:

→ Revenue is actually recognized correctly
→ Cash balances are real
→ Close processes are tight
→ Controls actually work

It’s not exciting work. But it’s the reason the board trusts the numbers.

2. Practical judgement (not textbook accounting)

Bad controllers follow rules blindly.

Great controllers understand why the rules exist.

In real companies, situations are rarely clean:

→ A contract clause doesn’t perfectly fit revenue guidance
→ A vendor invoice lands after the close
→ A deal structure sits in a grey area

You can’t run finance like an exam paper.

Controllers need judgment:

✅ When to be strict
✅ When to be practical
✅ When to push back
✅ When to help the business move forward safely

Perfect numbers are good. Sound judgment is better. Because accounting standards don’t run your business. People do.

1. Ownership of the numbers

Modern finance tools are powerful. But they also create distance.

→ Dashboards update automatically.
→ Reports generate instantly.
→ AI drafts the explanation.

But when something goes wrong, tools won’t stand in the boardroom. The controller/CFO will.

Great controllers know how the numbers are built:

✅ What sits inside each account
✅ Where the estimates come from
✅ Which assumptions matter

They don’t hide behind systems. They own the numbers. Because when finance loses accountability, the entire company loses trust in the data.

A message I got yesterday reminded me why honesty matters more than money. 

Someone connected with me yesterday. His profile was admirable — CTO title at some AI company, A Master of Science in IT and PhD in Computer Science, all from Harvard. Ex Tech lead at Atlassian(Jira) and ex SWE at IBM. 
I admit that I was really excited to accept his connection. I mean, who wouldn't? Just look at his titles above. 

We all know the problem. US companies need talent. Talented developers exist all over the world. But hiring internationally is complicated — visas, legal paperwork, sponsorship. It's a real headache for everyone involved.

So what was this Harvard PhD's groundbreaking solution to one of the biggest challenges in global tech hiring? Simple. Give international developers fake US citizen profiles. Let them interview as someone else. Get hired. Do the work. Split the money 50/50.

PhD-level problem solving right there. Harvard must be so proud.
I have to say, the business model is creative. You fake your own profile to look trustworthy. Then you recruit real developers to fake their profiles. Then those developers fake their way through interviews at real companies. If there was a PhD in fraud, this guy would actually deserve one.

Of course, let's be honest. No real Harvard graduate is spending their evenings on LinkedIn, pitching identity fraud to strangers. The degree is fake. The title is fake. The name and picture are probably borrowed from some stock photo. The only thing this man built from scratch is the scam.

Yes, the gap between international talent and US companies is a real problem. But the answer is building legitimate bridges — proper agencies, visa sponsorship, remote contracts. Not stealing identities. Not making honest developers into criminals. Not whatever this is.

That's a crime with extra steps.
Sometimes it's just a well-dressed scam with a PhD.

Over the past year, one thing has become very clear — #GCCs in India are not hiring the way they used to.

It’s no longer about scale. It’s about precision.

We are seeing a strong shift from volume hiring to building lean, high-impact teams. Roles are fewer, but expectations are much higher. Companies are investing in people who can solve problems, take ownership, and actually move the needle.

A few interesting patterns I have been noticing:

→ Skills are starting to matter more than degrees
→ AI is quietly becoming a big part of how candidates are screened
→ Tier-2 cities are gaining real traction (not just as a backup option anymore)
→ Fresher hiring is coming back—but very targeted (AI, data, core tech roles)
→ Contract + RPO models are becoming a go-to for scaling teams efficiently

Also, despite all the noise around layoffs, GCCs have actually outpaced traditional IT firms in hiring this year.

But here’s the real challenge:

It’s not a talent shortage. It’s a talent quality and fit problem.

The gap between what’s on paper and what shows up in interviews is still one of the biggest bottlenecks in hiring today.
For companies that are getting this right, hiring is no longer just an HR function, it’s a strategic advantage.
And for the rest, this shift is going to be hard to ignore.

Curious to hear — are you seeing the same trends in your hiring or job search experience?

** Unemployed for 1 year: What's it like, and what can you do **

By this May, I will have been without a job for a year. If you'd have asked me how long it would take to secure a new job after redundancy in 2025, I would have said about 6 months, worst-case.

But then reality hit. I discovered that when you're down there, it is a new low that nobody in a stable job sees or has any awareness of.

I am writing this post to offer some empathy to others who I know are in the same boat.

The situation is demoralising to say the least. A year of this tests your nerves and resilience like nothing else.

The impact on your financial situation is catastrophic. Your pride has to be suspended, as you will need to ask for favours and beg and borrow, and it might take years to recover.

Regarding a new job, you clutch at straws; literally any positive comment anyone you're talking to presents a lifeline, and you grab at it. This is because on the inside, you are sweating over how you are going to meet this month's bills and save your home.

You have to sacrifice things:

- Shop in budget and discount grocery stores only
- Stop using your car. In fact, sell it, or put it off-road
- Reduce your mortgage to interest-only
- Cancel all your subscriptions. Yes, Netflix. Yes, Amazon. Yes, the gym
- Cancel your clubs and going out to see friends
- Get rid of your contract mobile phone and go SIM-only
- Forget about going on holiday
- Tell the kids Santa won't be bringing much at Christmas and on their birthdays. (No, it isn't because they've been naughty)

For most people, the above are just things you have or do. But they become acutely painful when you are scratching around for an income.

At the same time, you have to fight the recruitment merry-go-round:

- Endless job applications that take hours
- LinkedIn jobs that aren't live anymore
- Competing with others who use AI to apply
- Multi-stage hiring processes
- Lower salary bandings than you are used to
- Unconfirmable ageism?

I am now in my early 50s, and this is getting much harder. When I am at an age where my 30+ years of business experience is of great value somewhere, it feels like the "system" is overlooking all of this. So I study, do gig work and record music at home. I also qualified with an MBA in Customering - that was a huge positive.

So if you have experienced anything similar, I feel you. Waking up every day and forgetting about everything, before you then remember where you are, is a daily battle.

If you are not in this situation, I give you this advice: NEVER take anything for granted. If you have a secure job and a roof over your head, you are a VERY LUCKY sausage.

I am sure there is a light at the end of the tunnel. But in this situation, it is time that goes against us.

Heads up - you will get there. And by all means DM me if you would like a chat about anything.

🚨 SECRET: Your Agency’s Hidden Agenda for Website Maintenance

I'm Habib Ahmed, CEO of Websloop Digital Agency, with over 9 years of experience working with and against agencies. I've seen firsthand how agencies manipulate their clients to increase profits. Today, I'm exposing the dirty secret behind website maintenance contracts.

Agencies often use a tactic called "block billing" to inflate their maintenance costs. This means they charge you a fixed fee for a certain number of hours, regardless of how much work is actually done. As a result, you might end up paying for hours that are not even used.

For example, let's say your agency charges you $500 per month for website maintenance. If they only work on your website for 10 hours, but charge you for 20 hours, you're essentially paying for 10 extra hours of work that didn't happen. This can add up quickly, and you might be surprised at how much you're overpaying.

To avoid falling victim to this tactic, make sure to ask your agency for a breakdown of their hours and costs. Check if they're charging you for hours that are not actually worked. You can also consider hiring an external auditor to review your agency's invoices.

Did your agency use block billing to inflate their maintenance costs? Let me know in the comments.

💡 Tip: Always review your agency's invoices carefully and ask for a breakdown of their hours and costs.

Contrarian View: AI doesn't just kill some jobs.
It kills the idea of a “complete company”.

Most founders still design scaling companies like it’s 2005:

One monolithic organisation.
All functions in-house.
Hiring full-time for capabilities they need 20% of the time.

You don’t need to own the whole stack.

➡️ Internal teams for sustained, high-stakes decisions
➡️ AI agents for repeatable, data-heavy tasks
➡️ Specialist partners for capital-intensive or highly regulated modules
➡️ Contract networks for demand spikes and highly specialized expertise

1️⃣ Burn aligned with proven value, not fixed overhead
2️⃣ Capital re-routed quickly when data or regulation shifts
3️⃣ Risk ring-fenced by module instead of betting the whole company

AI didn’t just change workflows. It changed what a company is.

AI PMs: your PRD is not the most important document you will write this year.

Your eval suite is.

Here is why that shift matters, and what it looks like in practice 👇

A PRD defines what to build. It describes the feature, the user story, the acceptance criteria. It is a specification written before the product exists.

An eval suite defines what good looks like once AI is running. It is a set of test cases that tell you, systematically, whether your AI feature is doing what you intended. Not once. Every single time it changes.

The difference is not just format. It is timing, ownership and risk.

With traditional software, a PRD written upfront holds most of its value throughout the build. The feature either works or it does not. Testing is binary.

With AI, outputs are probabilistic. The same input produces different outputs. A prompt change that improves one scenario quietly breaks three others. Without evals, you find out from a customer. With evals, you find out before you ship.

Here is a real example.

Imagine you are a PM building an AI contract review feature for a B2B legal SaaS. Your AI reads uploaded contracts and flags risk clauses.

Your PRD says: "The AI should identify clauses that create unlimited liability."

That sounds clear. But what does it actually mean in practice?

Your eval suite answers that:

Test 1: Contract with explicit unlimited liability clause. Expected output: flagged. Result: pass.

Test 2: Contract with liability cap waiver buried in an appendix. Expected output: flagged. Result: fail.

Test 3: Contract in plain English with no legal formatting. Expected output: flagged. Result: pass.

Test 4: Contract with liability clause referencing another document. Expected output: flagged with caveat. Result: fail.

The PRD told the team what to build. The eval suite told them whether it actually works across the messy, real-world inputs that legal teams will throw at it.

That is the gap AI PMs need to own.

Writing the eval suite is the new act of product thinking. It forces you to enumerate the edge cases, define what success actually looks like and build a feedback loop that catches regressions before users do.

If you are building AI features and you do not yet have an eval suite, you do not yet have a quality bar.

Are you writing evals or still relying on manual QA? 👇

Who is #responsible for the #actions of #ai #agents? Responsibility for AI agents is currently a shared human burden, as #AI #systems do not have #legal personhood and cannot be held liable for their actions. Responsibility is typically distributed across the following key stakeholders: 
Key Responsible Parties
> The #Deployer (Organization/User): The primary #legal and #ethical #responsibility falls on the entity or individual that puts the AI agent into circulation. For instance, if a company uses an agent for customer service or hiring, that #company is #responsible for any #errors, discrimination, or breaches the agent commits.
> The #Developer: Human engineers and tech companies (like OpenAI, Google, or Anthropic) are responsible for the foundational model's #safety, training data #quality, and addressing foreseeable #risks.
> The #Agent Manager: A new role emerging in businesses (often under a Chief AI Officer) tasked with orchestrating how agents work, ensuring they remain aligned with #business #goals, and conducting regular #audits of their performance.
> The Platform #Provider: Companies providing the infrastructure or cloud services (like Amazon Web Services (AWS) or Google Cloud) are responsible for secure access #controls and the #governance #infrastructure that allows agents to function. 

Emerging Legal Frameworks
Governments are actively refining laws to address the "#AI #responsibility #gap": 
#EuropeanUnion: The AI Liability Directive (March 2026) alleviates the burden of proof for victims of autonomous harm, presuming fault lies with the deployer if they cannot demonstrate proper oversight.
#UnitedStates: Responsibility is often handled through existing tort law, such as negligence or product liability, though new directives emphasize industry self-regulation and "light-touch" oversight.
#Singapore & #Japan: These nations have published specific governance frameworks and "soft law" guidelines to help companies manage risk through contracts and best practices.

Midlands IT & Engineering Labour Market – March 2026 (KPMG/REC Report on Jobs)

The latest KPMG and REC Midlands data is out, and if you're hiring or job-seeking in IT, software, or engineering across our region, it's worth paying attention.

The headlines:

Permanent placements fell at the steepest rate in five months (index: 45.4). 

For IT and engineering hiring managers, that means longer sign-off processes, frozen reqs, and a cautious "wait and see" approach to team growth.

But temp billings surged to 57.8 - eight consecutive months of growth, and the Midlands was the only English region still expanding. 

Skills shortages persist:

The report flags Software Developers, IT, Technical Roles, Engineers, Specialist Engineering, and Automation Testers as skills in short supply. 

That mirrors what I see daily - whether it's C#/.NET developers, cybersecurity engineers, HPC specialists, or controls engineers, genuine technical talent remains hard to find even as overall candidate numbers rise.

And that rising availability needs context. 

More people are on the market, but in IT and engineering, volume doesn't equal quality. 

The influx is creating more noise - more AI-generated CVs, more speculative applications - making it harder to identify the right people without a technically literate recruitment partner who can cut through it.

Pay is shifting too; permanent starting salary growth is at its weakest in five years, but temp/contract rates are climbing. In specialist technical areas, day rates are holding firm.

My take: 

The market is cautious, but demand for strong technical talent hasn't disappeared. If you're hiring, the pool is better than it's been in years, but the best people still won't wait. 

If you're a tech professional, now's the time to sharpen your CV and skills narrative, not rely on job boards.

“Stop treating freelancers like vendors. The moment you see them as an extension of your team, everything changes.”


That was one of the main points that Jamie Read highlighted on The Independent Workforce podcast!

When the procurement team works with freelancers, they want 20 hours for the price of 1. They bargain like crazy because they are used to working with buying software and hardware, not humans.

But actually, freelancers are not just an “extra-hands-SaaS.”
And working with them is not just a cost decision.
It’s a strategy decision 💪


And here are 3 thoughts for both sides to make this decision better 👇

🛠 For companies benefiting from freelancers:

1️⃣ You’re not just buying hours. You’re bringing in a superpower.
The best freelancers bring experience, perspective, and solutions you don’t have internally.

2️⃣ Your inner systems might be the real problem.
If hiring freelancers feels hard, it’s often because procurement treats them like vendors, not people. That kills speed and quality. And humanness.

3️⃣ Freelancers also choose.
The best talent doesn’t stick around for bad processes, late payments, or a lack of respect. Treat them well, or lose them to competitors 🤷‍♂️


🤩 For freelancers supercharing clients:

1️⃣ Think like a business, not a worker.
Contracts, positioning, clarity. This is what makes companies trust you.

2️⃣ You’re building relationships, not just delivering tasks.
The real growth comes from repeat work and long-term partnerships.

3️⃣ Stability comes from a pipeline, not just one client.
One client is risk. A pipeline is security.


TL;DR
The companies that win won’t be the ones with the biggest teams, but the ones that know how to blend full-time and independent talent. And AI 😉


If you want to learn how to actually do that, go check the full conversation with Jamie Read in the comments 🤓👇



P.S. If you’re a company looking to hire freelancers—or a freelancer ready to find your next project—check out freelancermap (international) 😎

What's shaping in-house legal resourcing in Australia? Our Senior Client Development Manager, Nina Fotheringham, shares three trends every GC and Head of Legal should have on their radar:
 
📊 Blended workforce models are here to stay: with ongoing cost pressures, legal teams are mixing permanent, fixed-term and contract talent to manage busy periods without adding fixed headcount. But as more contractors seek permanent roles for security, expect longer lead times to secure flexible talent.
 
🤖 AI fluency is non-negotiable: teams are moving beyond pilots to governed AI workflows. The focus? Upskilling, hiring for tech fluency, and redesigning roles around productivity. If you're competing for top talent, a visible innovation roadmap matters.
 
🏢 Hybrid resets are shrinking talent pools: stricter onsite expectations (three to five days) and limited relocation support are reducing available talent, particularly outside Sydney and Melbourne.

Legal tech in 2026: everyone's building AI for lawyers. And I mean everyone. (Including Anthropic, apparently)

- Tools to make lawyers faster 
- Tools to make research easier 
- Tools to automate discovery 

All useful. Really. But here's what we're mostly missing:

 The 98 % of businesses that don't have the time or resources hire a legal team in the first place.

- A founder hiring their first employee doesn't need AI-powered legal research. They need an employment contract. Today. For less than €1,500.
- A 10-person startup navigating GDPR doesn't need a better dashboard for lawyers. They need compliance sorted without dropping €15,000 and waiting two month.

Making lawyers 10x more productive is great. But if you still have to pay humans to communicate via email like it was 1971, and getting things done takes weeks, it doesn't solve the access problem.

Finance: triage claims/arrears, pre-fill packs, flag fraud; faster decisions and lower loss ratios.
Small business: automate quotes, onboarding and invoices from emails; fewer admin hours and quicker cash collection.
Operations/Retail: Gousto-style forecasting cuts waste; Ocado-style routing improves fulfilment at scale.
Manufacturing: Rolls‑Royce-style predictive maintenance lifts uptime and margin.
Professional Services: draft first-pass reports/contracts and summarise evidence; more billable capacity without hiring.

From LLMs to hallucinations The AI Vocabulary Every Business Professional Needs to Know — A Complete Guide

Most professionals are making AI decisions every day — but how many actually understand what LLMs, hallucinations, or inference really mean?

AI jargon is no longer just for engineers. It shapes vendor contracts, product strategy, hiring decisions, and risk management across every industry.

Here's a complete, plain-English breakdown of every major AI term you need to know in 2026 👇

#ArtificialIntelligence #AI #LLM #LLMs #hallucinations

90% of contractors who try AI give up after one attempt. I know why.

They uploaded some drawings, typed "estimate this," and got rubbish back. So they wrote it off as a gimmick.

But the output was rubbish for a very specific reason: they gave the AI nothing to work with.

No about-me file. No past estimates. No context about their sector, their margins, or how they structure their pricing.

That's like hiring a graduate QS on Monday morning and saying "price this job" without telling them what company they work for, what sector you're in, or what your standard prelims percentage is.

You'd get generic nonsense back. Same thing with AI.

The fix is a text file that takes two minutes to write. I call it the about-me file. Here's what goes in it:

↳ Who you are and what your business does
↳ What sector you typically work in (commercial, residential, mixed)
↳ What size jobs you normally price
↳ Your typical prelims percentage
↳ Your standard overheads and profit percentage
↳ Any standard exclusions you always include

Here's mine as an example:

"I'm a fit-out contractor based in Manchester. We do Cat A to Cat B commercial fit-outs, typically 200-1000m2. I price using UK rates. Prelims usually X% depending on contract length. Overheads and profit at X%. We exclude furniture, IT infrastructure, and signage unless specified."

That's it. Save it in your project folder. Tell the AI to read it before starting the estimate.

The difference is immediate. Without context, the AI uses generic rates and guesses your structure. With context, it calibrates. The rates are closer. The prelims make sense. The trade breakdown matches how you actually price.

If you tried AI estimating once and thought it was a waste of time, this is almost certainly why. The tool wasn't the problem. The brief was.

Write the file. Run the same job again. Compare the two outputs. The difference will surprise you.

🇨🇭 Something shifted in the Swiss data engineering market this quarter, and most job seekers haven’t noticed yet.
Three weeks of scanning roles across Zürich, Geneva, Basel, and Zug. Here’s what Swiss employers are actually asking for in 2026 by and it’s not what the 2023 playbook told you.

What’s hot right now 🔥
➡️ Lakehouse is the default, not the future. Databricks + Delta Lake or Snowflake + Iceberg now appear in the majority of senior DE postings. Pure “SQL developer” roles are fading fast.
➡️ dbt has quietly become non-negotiable. If you’re not shipping transformations with dbt, you’re competing for a shrinking pool of legacy ETL roles.

➡️ AI/LLM-adjacent pipelines are the new premium. Vector stores, RAG data prep, feature pipelines for ML, Swiss pharma (Basel) and finance (Zürich, Geneva) are paying top-of-band for this.
➡️ Data contracts & governance. Regulated industries (FINMA, pharma, insurance) want engineers who think about lineage, schema evolution, and quality SLAs, not just pipelines that “run.”

➡️ Streaming is no longer a nice-to-have. Kafka + Flink show up in trading, IoT, and real-time personalization roles far more than a year ago.

The uncomfortable truth 💬
Swiss unemployment sits near 2.2%. Companies are fighting for talent, but hiring has become selective, not desperate. “Quality over quantity” is the 2026 mantra. Employers want engineers who deliver business value from week one.

Salary signal 💰
Experienced data engineers in CH are landing CHF 120k–150k, with senior/lead roles crossing CHF 160k+ in finance and pharma hubs.

My take 🎯
The data engineer who wins in Switzerland in 2026 is not the one with the longest tool list. It’s the one who can architect for cost, prove data quality, and speak the business language, ideally in German, French or English too.

Question for you 👇
CEOs, hiring managers, fellow engineers, what’s the one skill you’re prioritizing in your next data hire? Drop it in the comments. Curious to see where the Swiss market really lands.
